explanation:
The statement "prop_0 by prop_1 once prop_2 (without prop_3)" describes an event that must eventually occur when a specific set of conditions are met. 
- "once" indicates that an event must happen at some point in the future, which translates to the LTL operator `F` (Eventually).
- The condition "prop_2 (without prop_3)" means that `prop_2` must be true and `prop_3` must be false simultaneously. This translates to `prop_2 & !prop_3`.
- "prop_0 by prop_1" suggests that `prop_0` is achieved or accompanied by `prop_1`. In the context of a single eventual event, this implies that `prop_0` and `prop_1` are both true at the moment the conditions are met. This translates to `prop_0 & prop_1`.
- Combining these parts, the LTL formula asserts that eventually, `prop_2`, `!prop_3`, `prop_0`, and `prop_1` will all be true at the same point in time. The phrases "the air supply, and the power source" are not provided as atomic propositions and therefore cannot be explicitly represented in the LTL formula.

The phrase "once prop_2" implies that `prop_2` must eventually become true, serving as a precondition. This is captured by the outer `F(prop_2 & ...)`. The phrase "prop_0 by prop_1" indicates that when `prop_0` occurs, `prop_1` must also be true at that same time. This is represented by the conjunction `(prop_0 & prop_1)`. The inner `F(...)` signifies that `prop_0` and `prop_1` will eventually occur together after `prop_2` has become true. Therefore, the LTL formula `F(prop_2 & F(prop_0 & prop_1))` accurately translates to "eventually, `prop_2` is true, and from that point onwards, eventually `prop_0` and `prop_1` will be true simultaneously."

The phrase "prop_0 by prop_1 once prop_2 (without prop_3)" is interpreted to mean that eventually (F), prop_0 occurs. At the moment prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must also be true, and the condition that prop_2 is true while prop_3 is false (prop_2 & !prop_3) must also hold. The additional phrases "the air supply" and "the power source" are not represented by the provided atomic propositions and thus are not included in the LTL formula. Therefore, the formula asserts that there will be a future state where prop_0, prop_1, prop_2, and not prop_3 are all true simultaneously.

The statement implies two key conditions. First, the Start-up Mode (initiated by prop_0) can only occur when prop_1 (representing the air supply and power source being attached) is true. This is captured by the LTL formula G(prop_0 -> prop_1), meaning that globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true. Second, it implies an obligation: once prop_1 is true, the Start-up Mode must eventually be initiated by prop_0. This is captured by the LTL formula G(prop_1 -> F prop_0), meaning that globally, if prop_1 is true, then eventually prop_0 must become true. These two conditions together ensure that prop_0 acts as the initiator only when prop_1 is met, and that prop_0 will eventually initiate once prop_1 is met.

The statement "prop_0 by prop_1 once prop_2" implies a sequence of events triggered by a condition. 
1.  "Once prop_2": This indicates that `prop_2` (all connections are attached) is a trigger. The LTL operator `G(prop_2 -> ...)` expresses that whenever `prop_2` becomes true, a certain sequence of events must eventually follow.
2.  "prop_0 by prop_1": The rationale clarifies that `prop_0` (turning on the system) is done "so" `prop_1` (sensors and valves can be properly tested) can happen. This means `prop_0` must occur, and subsequently, `prop_1` must occur. This temporal ordering is captured by `F (prop_0 & F prop_1)`, meaning eventually `prop_0` is true, and from that point, eventually `prop_1` is true. 
Combining these, the LTL formula `G(prop_2 -> F (prop_0 & F prop_1))` states: "Always, if `prop_2` is true, then eventually `prop_0` will be true, and after `prop_0`, `prop_1` will eventually be true."

The phrase "once prop_1 (without prop_2)" identifies the precondition for initiation. This translates to `prop_1 & !prop_2`. The phrase "The Start-up Mode shall be initiated by prop_0" indicates that `prop_0` will eventually become true to initiate the mode. The word "once" implies a temporal sequence where the condition must be met, and then the initiation follows. The LTL formula `F(A & F B)` captures this perfectly: "Eventually, A is true, and from that point onwards, B will eventually be true." Therefore, the formula becomes `F((prop_1 & !prop_2) & F prop_0)`. Phrases like "the air supply, and the power source" are not translated as they are not provided as atomic propositions.

The formula states that until `prop_2` and `prop_3` are both true, neither `prop_2` nor `prop_3` can be true. Once `prop_2` and `prop_3` become true (representing all connections attached), it must globally hold from that point onwards that if `prop_1` is true, then `prop_0` will be true in the next state. This captures that `prop_0` is enabled by `prop_1` only after the preconditions (`prop_2` and `prop_3`) are met.

The phrase "prop_0 by prop_1" is interpreted as the simultaneous occurrence of `prop_0` and `prop_1` (`prop_0 & prop_1`). The phrase "once prop_2" indicates two main conditions: first, that this combined event (`prop_0 & prop_1`) must eventually occur; and second, that this event can only occur after `prop_2` has become true. This is expressed by ensuring `prop_0 & prop_1` is false until `prop_2` becomes true (`!(prop_0 & prop_1) U prop_2`), and that `prop_0 & prop_1` eventually becomes true (`F (prop_0 & prop_1)`). Combined, these ensure `prop_2` eventually occurs, and `(prop_0 & prop_1)` occurs only after `prop_2` has occurred.

The requirement "prop_1 (without prop_2), prop_3, and prop_4" is first translated into a combined condition C: (prop_1 & !prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4). The phrase "The Start-up Mode shall be initiated by prop_0 once C" implies two conditions: 1. Obligation: If condition C is met, then prop_0 must eventually occur to initiate the mode. This is expressed as G(C -> F prop_0). 2. Precondition/Safety: prop_0 (the initiation) should only occur if condition C is true, preventing premature initiation. This is expressed as G(prop_0 -> C). The conjunction of these two LTL formulas captures the full meaning.

The phrase "prop_0 by prop_1" is interpreted as the conjunction of prop_0 and prop_1 (i.e., prop_0 & prop_1), meaning they occur together. The phrase "once prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4" implies that the conjunction of these three propositions (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) must eventually occur. The entire statement means that after prop_2, prop_3, and prop_4 have all become true, then eventually prop_0 and prop_1 will become true together. This is captured by the LTL formula F ( (prop_2 & prop_3 & prop_4) & F (prop_0 & prop_1) ).
